chapter_summary: This is the introductory part of the Mimamsaslokavarttika by Kumarila Bhatta, with Sucaritamisra's Kasika commentary. It contains technical notes on transliteration and text encoding, but no philosophical content or narrative.

story.title: Introduction to Mimamsaslokavarttika
story.arc: No narrative arc; this is a metadata section.
story.timeline_note: Not applicable.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana) and its relationship to perception (pratyaksha). It argues that inference, like perception, is reliable and does not require separate examination because it is based on known relationships. The commentary by Sucaritamisra explains that the purpose is not to define pramanas in an unconventional way but to reaffirm their worldly acceptance, countering doubts about their validity.

story.title: Defense of Inference as a Valid Pramana
story.arc: The text establishes that inference, like perception, is self-evident and does not require further validation, countering skeptical objections.
story.timeline_note: Commentary on Mimamsa Sutras, likely post-5th century CE.
story.comic_potential: A debate between a skeptic and a Mimamsaka, with the latter using logical arguments to prove the obvious.

teachings.teaching: Inference is reliable because it is based on a known relationship (sambandha) and does not deviate from perception.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that valid knowledge arises from direct experience (pratyaksha) and logical extension (anumana) without need for external validation, emphasizing self-reliance in spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (part 3) discusses the nature of inference (anumana) and the relationship between words and their meanings. It addresses objections regarding the qualification of inferred entities, the role of expectation (akanksha), and the proper interpretation of compound words like tatpurusha. The text argues that inference is valid when based on a known relation, and that even subordinate elements can be qualified if they fulfill an expectation.

story.title: Mimamsa Sutras — Chapter 0 (part 3): Inference and Word Relations
story.arc: Dialectical examination of inference and syntactic qualification
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely from the 3rd-2nd century BCE.
story.comic_potential: Low; dense philosophical debate with no narrative action.

teachings.teaching: Inference is based on a known relation (avinabhava) between the sign and the signified.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that knowledge arises from established connections in practice; the yogi infers the subtle from the gross through disciplined observation.

teachings.teaching: A word that is subordinate (upasarjana) can still be qualified if it fulfills an expectation (akanksha).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as analogous to the subordinate role of the body in yoga; though secondary, it must be properly qualified through practice to serve the higher self.

teachings.teaching: In a tatpurusha compound, the latter word is primary; the former qualifies it.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the primacy of the inner self (purusha) over external actions; actions qualify the self but do not define it.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the interpretation of compound words (samasa) and the role of the genitive case (sasthi) in determining the primary and secondary elements. It argues that the primary meaning is conveyed by the latter part of the compound, and that the genitive case indicates a relation of invariable concomitance (vyapya-vyapaka). The text also addresses the use of inference (anumana) in understanding compounds, emphasizing that the meaning is grasped through conventional usage and context.

story.title: Interpretation of Compounds and Genitive Case
story.arc: Exegetical analysis of Sanskrit grammar and semantics
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, a foundational text of the Mimamsa school of Hindu philosophy.

teachings.teaching: The primary meaning in a compound is determined by the latter part (uttarapada), and the genitive case indicates a relation of invariable concomitance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this reflects the principle that the ultimate reality (Brahman) is the primary substratum, and all other elements are secondary and dependent on it.

teachings.teaching: Inference (anumana) is based on the perception of a part (ekadesa) of the invariable concomitant (vyapya), leading to knowledge of the invariable concommitant (vyapaka).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of using sensory perception as a means to infer the subtle energies and ultimately the Self.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the concept of 'jñātasambandha' (known relation) in inference, particularly focusing on the relationship between the vyāpya (pervaded) and vyāpaka (pervader). It examines how a known relation between two entities allows inference of one from the other, even when only a part is perceived. The text explores different interpretations of compound words like 'jñātasambandha' and debates whether the relation itself or the relata are primary. It uses examples like smoke and fire to illustrate that repeated observation establishes invariable concomitance, enabling inference of the whole from a part.

story.title: Analysis of Known Relation in Inference
story.arc: Exposition of Mimamsa epistemology regarding inference based on known relations.
story.timeline_note: This is a philosophical text; no narrative timeline.

teachings.teaching: Inference is based on a known relation (jñātasambandha) between the vyāpya and vyāpaka, established through repeated observation of their co-occurrence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would see this as analogous to the practitioner's repeated experience of the connection between action (kriya) and its result, leading to reliable knowledge.

teachings.teaching: Even when only a part (ekadeśa) is perceived, the whole (samudāyin) can be inferred if the relation is known.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to inferring the complete state of inner purification from a single observed practice or sign.

teachings.teaching: The compound 'jñātasambandha' can be interpreted as a karmadhāraya (apposition) or as a bahuvrīhi (possessive) compound, affecting which entity is considered primary.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might note that the correct interpretation depends on the context of practice, where the relation itself becomes the focus.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana). It argues that inference is based on a relation of invariable concomitance (avinabhava) between the probans and the probandum, which can be established through causation (karyakaranabhava) or identity (tadatmya). The text refutes objections regarding the inference of qualities like color from taste, explaining that they share a common cause.

story.title: Discussion on Inference
story.arc: Philosophical debate on the validity and method of inference
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.

teachings.teaching: Inference is based on invariable concomitance, which can be established through causation or identity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that knowledge arises from direct perception of the relation between cause and effect, akin to the yogic insight into the unity of all things.

teachings.teaching: The invariable relation is not established by mere observation of agreement and difference, but by understanding the nature of the things themselves.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that true knowledge comes from direct experience (pratyaksha) and not from mere logical deduction, aligning with the yogic path of direct realization.

teachings.teaching: Even qualities like color can be inferred from taste when they share a common cause, illustrating the interconnectedness of all phenomena.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga view that all aspects of reality are linked through a common substratum (Brahman), and one can perceive the whole through any part.

chapter_summary: This chapter presents a debate on the nature of inference (anumāna) in the context of Mīmāṃsā epistemology. It critiques the Nyāya view that invariable concomitance (vyāpti) is known through perception or inference, arguing that such knowledge is impossible due to the indeterminacy of causal relations and the lack of a reliable means to establish universal concomitance. The text emphasizes that inference based on cause-effect or identity relations is flawed, and that the only valid inference is from a property to its substratum (liṅga-liṅgin relation).

story.title: Critique of Inference in Mīmāṃsā
story.arc: The chapter systematically refutes the Nyāya theory of inference by showing that invariable concomitance cannot be established through perception or inference, and that the only valid inference is from a mark to its substratum.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mīmāṃsā Sūtras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.
story.comic_potential: A debate between two philosophers arguing about the validity of inference, with the Mīmāṃsaka using analogies like fire and smoke to challenge the Naiyāyika.

teachings.teaching: Inference based on cause-effect or identity relations is invalid because such relations cannot be known with certainty.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the idea that direct experience (pratyakṣa) is superior to inference, and that the ultimate truth is realized through direct perception in meditation, not through logical deduction.

teachings.teaching: The only valid inference is from a property (liṅga) to its substratum (liṅgin), as in the case of smoke indicating fire.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the direct perception of the Self (liṅgin) through the practice of pratyāhāra and dhyāna, where the mind (liṅga) is used to infer the presence of the Self.

teachings.teaching: Invariable concomitance (vyāpti) cannot be known through perception or inference, as it requires knowledge of all instances, which is impossible.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would see this as a limitation of intellectual knowledge, emphasizing that the yogi transcends such limitations by directly experiencing the unity of all existence.

chapter_summary: This chapter discusses the nature of inference (anumāna) in Mīmāṃsā epistemology, focusing on the relationship between vyāpya (pervaded) and vyāpaka (pervader). It argues that inference requires knowledge of a universal relation (vyāpti) between the probans and probandum, and that the probans must be a vyāpya of the probandum. The text critiques the idea that perception alone can establish such relations due to variations in time, place, and form.

story.title: Mīmāṃsā on Inference and Vyāpti
story.arc: Epistemological analysis of inference, establishing the necessity of a universal relation between probans and probandum.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mīmāṃsā Sūtras, likely from the 5th chapter (pāda 4).

teachings.teaching: Inference requires knowledge of a universal relation (vyāpti) between the probans and probandum; the probans must be a vyāpya (pervaded) of the probandum.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that inner realization (vyāpya) must be grounded in the universal consciousness (vyāpaka) for valid knowledge.

teachings.teaching: Perception alone cannot establish vyāpti due to variations in time, place, and form.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this highlights that sensory experience is limited and cannot grasp the universal; only through meditative insight (sākṣī) can one perceive the invariant relation.

teachings.teaching: The relation between probans and probandum is not merely co-occurrence but a necessary connection; the probans cannot exist without the probandum.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the individual self (jīva) is pervaded by the Supreme Self (Brahman) and cannot exist independently.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (Adhyaya 5, Pada 4) discusses the nature of inference (anumana), focusing on the roles of vyapya (pervaded) and vyapaka (pervader). It argues that the logical ground for inference is vyapyatva (being pervaded), not vyapakatva (being pervader), and addresses objections regarding the relationship between the two, including spatial and temporal considerations.

story.title: The Nature of Inference in Mimamsa
story.arc: Philosophical analysis of the logical relation between vyapya and vyapaka in inference.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.

teachings.teaching: The logical ground for inference is vyapyatva (being pervaded), not vyapakatva (being pervader).
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the practitioner should focus on the observable effect (vyapya) to infer the cause (vyapaka), akin to using the body's sensations to infer the presence of prana.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti (invariable concomitance) is a relation of necessary connection, not physical contact.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the non-physical, causal link between inner states and outer practices; e.g., the regularity of breath (vyapya) leads to the inference of prana (vyapaka) without direct contact.

teachings.teaching: The same relation of vyapya and vyapaka applies regardless of spatial or temporal distance, as long as the necessary connection holds.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this supports the idea that the effects of yogic practices (vyapya) can be used to infer the presence of subtle energies (vyapaka) even across time and space.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (part 10 of Chapter 0) discusses the nature of vyapti (invariable concomitance) as a necessary condition for inference (anumana). It examines whether vyapti can be established through perception (pratyaksha), inference itself, verbal testimony (shabda), comparison (upamana), or presumption (arthapatti). The text argues that vyapti cannot be known through any of these means individually, and raises the problem of how inference is possible if vyapti is not established. It explores the role of repeated observation (bhuyodarshana) and the formation of mental impressions (samskara) in generating the knowledge of vyapti, but ultimately questions the validity of such knowledge.

story.title: Examination of Vyapti in Mimamsa Epistemology
story.arc: The chapter presents a dialectical argument: the opponent claims vyapti cannot be known, and the text responds by considering various pramanas, ultimately leaving the question open.
story.timeline_note: This is part of a larger commentary on Mimamsa Sutras, likely from the 5th century CE or later.
story.comic_potential: The abstract debate about the foundation of inference could be visualized as a courtroom drama where each pramana is called as a witness to testify whether it can establish vyapti.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti (invariable concomitance) is the basis of inference, but its own validity is problematic.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this echoes the need for direct experiential knowledge (pratyaksha) through yogic perception to establish the invariable connection between cause and effect, which cannot be grasped by mere intellectual reasoning.

teachings.teaching: Repeated observation (bhuyodarshana) is proposed as a means to know vyapti, but it relies on memory and mental impressions (samskara), which are not direct perception.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the necessity of repeated practice (abhyasa) in Kriya-Yoga to create samskaras that lead to direct realization of the unity of all phenomena, transcending the duality of observer and observed.

teachings.teaching: No single pramana (means of knowledge) can establish vyapti; it requires a combination or a different approach.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that intellectual knowledge is insufficient; only through the integrated practice of Kriya-Yoga (union of action, devotion, and knowledge) can one attain the direct insight into the invariable principles underlying reality.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (part 11) discusses the nature of vyapti (invariable concomitance) and the means of establishing it. It argues that vyapti cannot be established through absence (abhava) because it is a positive relation, nor through mental perception (manasa) due to the mind's dependence on external senses. The chapter concludes that vyapti is self-evident in the first perception of a relation like fire and smoke, and is natural (svabhavika), thus not subject to deviation.

story.title: Establishing Invariable Concomitance
story.arc: Philosophical argument on the means of knowledge for vyapti
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, a foundational text of Purva Mimamsa
story.comic_potential: A debate between proponents of different pramanas, with witty refutations.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti cannot be established through absence (abhava) because it is a positive relation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that spiritual truths are positive realizations, not mere negations.

teachings.teaching: Mental perception (manasa) cannot establish vyapti because the mind is dependent on external senses.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as highlighting the need for direct experience (pratyaksha) in yoga, not mere mental speculation.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti is self-evident in the first perception of a relation and is natural (svabhavika), thus not subject to deviation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that the innate connection between practice and result is directly perceived in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana) and the establishment of invariable concomitance (vyapti). It argues that the relation between fire and smoke is natural (svabhavika) and not conditional (aupadhika), and that repeated observation (bhuyodarshana) is necessary to remove doubts about possible conditions. The text examines whether mere co-occurrence or a rule (niyama) is the basis for inference, and concludes that the relation must be understood through exegesis (vyakhyeya).

story.title: Establishing Invariable Concomitance in Inference
story.arc: The chapter argues that the relation between fire and smoke is natural, not conditional, and that repeated observation is necessary to remove doubts about possible conditions. It critiques alternative views and concludes that the relation must be understood through exegesis.
story.timeline_note: This is a philosophical discourse, not a narrative with a timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a dense philosophical argument.

teachings.teaching: The relation between fire and smoke is natural (svabhavika) and not conditional (aupadhika).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this corresponds to the principle that the connection between cause and effect is inherent and not imposed by external conditions, reflecting the natural order of dharma.

teachings.teaching: Repeated observation (bhuyodarshana) is necessary to remove doubts about possible conditions (upadhi).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the need for persistent practice (abhyasa) to purify the mind and remove obstacles (kleshas) that cloud perception of truth.

teachings.teaching: Inference (anumana) depends on invariable concomitance (vyapti), which cannot be established by perception or inference alone, but requires exegesis (vyakhyeya).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on scriptural study (svadhyaya) as a means to understand the deeper connections between actions and their results, beyond mere empirical observation.

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of vyapti (invariable concomitance) and how knowledge of it arises. It argues that vyapti is known through perception aided by repeated observation and mental impressions (samskaras), not through a separate pramana. The text refutes the possibility of non-apprehension, doubt, or error regarding vyapti, and explains the role of nirvikalpaka and savikalpaka perception in generating certainty.

story.title: Establishing the Perceptual Basis of Invariable Concomitance
story.arc: The chapter systematically refutes the idea that vyapti is unknown or doubtful, and argues that it is known through perception supported by repeated observation and mental impressions. It addresses objections regarding the role of nirvikalpaka and savikalpaka perception, and concludes that vyapti is directly perceived in the final instance of observation.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is a continuous argument without narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a dense philosophical debate with no dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: Knowledge of vyapti (invariable concomitance) arises from perception, not from a separate pramana. Repeated observation and mental impressions (samskaras) enable the final perception that reveals the invariable relation.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this corresponds to the process of abhyasa (repeated practice) leading to pratyabhijna (recognition) of the Self. The repeated observation of the mind's modifications (vrittis) eventually yields direct knowledge of the unchanging witness (sakshi).

teachings.teaching: The final perception of vyapti is a savikalpaka (determinate) perception that arises from a preceding nirvikalpaka (indeterminate) perception aided by samskaras.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the nirvikalpaka state corresponds to the stilling of all mental modifications (yogash chitta vritti nirodhah). The subsequent savikalpaka perception is the clear discernment (viveka) of the Self as distinct from the mind. The samskaras are the impressions from previous meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: Doubt and error regarding vyapti are impossible because the actual practice of inference (e.g., inferring fire from smoke) demonstrates certainty.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the direct experience of the Self through meditation removes all doubt and error about one's true nature. The repeated practice leads to unshakable conviction (nishchaya).

chapter_summary: This chapter of the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana) and the validity of the relation between smoke and fire. It argues that the invariable concomitance (vyapti) between smoke and fire is known through direct perception, not through inference itself. The text addresses objections about exceptions (vyabhicara) and defends the reliability of perception in establishing universal relations.

story.title: Establishing the Validity of Inference through Perception
story.arc: The chapter argues that the invariable relation between smoke and fire is perceived directly, not inferred, and that inference is based on such perceived relations. It addresses objections about exceptions and defends the reliability of perception in establishing universal connections.
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: The invariable concomitance between smoke and fire is known through direct perception, not through inference.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that direct experience (pratyaksha) of the relationship between cause and effect is foundational. The practitioner must directly perceive the connection between action (kriya) and result, not rely on abstract reasoning.

teachings.teaching: Exceptions to a rule (vyabhicara) are due to sub-species differences (avāntarajātibheda), not to the rule itself being invalid.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches that apparent contradictions in spiritual practice arise from differences in individual constitution or stage, not from the universal principles. The teacher must discern the sub-type to guide appropriately.

teachings.teaching: The relation of invariable concomitance is a positive entity (bhāvātmaka) that is perceived directly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the tangible, experiential link between practice and spiritual attainment, which is directly felt in meditation, not merely inferred.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of vyapti (invariable concomitance) and its role in inference (anumana). It argues that vyapti can be known through perception and scripture, and addresses objections about whether vyapti holds between particulars or universals. The text also examines the application of vyapti to sacrificial violence (himsa), distinguishing between prohibited and prescribed acts.

story.title: Vyapti and Inference in Mimamsa
story.arc: The chapter establishes the epistemological basis for vyapti, defends its perceptibility, and applies it to the case of sacrificial violence, showing that scriptural prescription overrides general prohibitions.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.
story.comic_potential: A debate between a Mimamsaka and an opponent about whether you can see the connection between fire and smoke, with the opponent insisting it's invisible.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti (invariable concomitance) can be known through perception, just as the capacities of other causal factors are known.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the idea that direct experience (pratyaksha) of causal relationships is possible through disciplined practice, not just inference.

teachings.teaching: The relationship between a means and its result is established by scripture when it is not perceptible.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that scriptural injunctions (vidhi) are necessary to reveal hidden connections, especially in spiritual practices where results are not immediately evident.

teachings.teaching: Vyapti can be between universals (sāmānya) rather than particulars, as in the case of fire and smoke.
teachings.lens_note: This supports the Kriya-Yoga view that universal principles underlie specific practices; understanding the universal allows application in diverse contexts.

teachings.teaching: In some cases, vyapti holds between particulars, as with the rising of Krittika and Rohini.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might see this as analogous to the precise sequence of actions in Kriya-Yoga, where specific practices lead to specific outcomes.

teachings.teaching: The general rule that himsa (violence) leads to adharma is overridden by scriptural prescription for sacrificial himsa.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle that divine command (as in Kriya-Yoga's guru's instruction) can supersede general ethical norms when it serves a higher purpose.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of dharma and adharma, focusing on the principle that prohibition (niṣedha) is the key factor in determining adharma, not the inherent nature of an act like hiṃsā (violence). It argues that even acts like hiṃsā can be dharma if prescribed by scripture, and that reasoning based on non-prohibitive factors leads to errors. The text also addresses logical fallacies, such as self-contradiction and conflict with scripture, and emphasizes the authority of Vedic injunction.

story.title: Mimamsa Sutras — Chapter 0 (part 16)
story.arc: Exposition of the role of prohibition in determining adharma, with refutation of opposing views.
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger philosophical treatise; no narrative timeline.

teachings.teaching: Adharma is determined by prohibition (niṣedha), not by the inherent nature of an act like hiṃsā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this underscores that the moral quality of an action depends on its alignment with scriptural injunction, not on a fixed essence. Practice must be guided by śāstra.

teachings.teaching: Even hiṃsā can be dharma if prescribed by scripture, as in animal sacrifice.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that Kriya-Yoga actions are to be performed as per śāstra, transcending personal notions of harm. The yogi acts without attachment, following divine command.

teachings.teaching: Arguments based on non-prohibitive factors are fallacious and lead to error.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on direct scriptural authority over speculative reasoning. In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner relies on the guru and śāstra, not personal logic.

teachings.teaching: Self-contradiction and conflict with scripture are signs of a flawed argument.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against ego-driven interpretations. Kriya-Yoga requires surrender to the tradition, avoiding intellectual pride.

teachings.teaching: The authority of Vedic injunction is paramount in determining dharma.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma, this is the bedrock of Kriya-Yoga: action must be performed as prescribed, with faith in the Vedas and the guru's instruction.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the eligibility (adhikara) for Vedic rituals, arguing that only the three twice-born varnas (Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas) are entitled, excluding Shudras. It also addresses the nature of inference (anumana) and the conditions for valid reasoning, emphasizing that only a direct connection (sambandha) can establish causality, not mere concomitance.

story.title: Eligibility for Vedic Rituals and the Nature of Inference
story.arc: The chapter first debates the adhikara for Vedic rituals, concluding that only the three twice-born varnas are eligible, not Shudras. Then it shifts to a discussion on inference, critiquing various fallacious arguments and establishing that a valid inference requires a direct causal connection, not mere concomitance.
story.timeline_note: This is a philosophical text; no narrative timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a dense philosophical treatise with no narrative or dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: Only the three twice-born varnas (Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas) are eligible for Vedic rituals; Shudras are excluded because they lack the requisite knowledge and fire.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, this teaching underscores the importance of proper initiation and preparation (upanayana) for spiritual practice. The exclusion of Shudras may be interpreted as a reminder that certain practices require specific qualifications, but the essence of yoga is accessible to all through devotion and service.

teachings.teaching: A valid inference must be based on a direct causal connection (sambandha), not on mere concomitance or accidental association.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct experience (pratyaksha) over inference. This teaching aligns with the idea that true knowledge comes from direct realization, not from logical deduction alone.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana), specifically how knowledge of one part (ekadesa) leads to knowledge of another part. It argues that the inferred entity (ekadesin) is both the object of inference and the means of inference, being one and the same. The text addresses objections about the unity of the inferred entity and uses examples like smoke and fire to illustrate the relationship.

story.title: Inference from Part to Part
story.arc: Philosophical analysis of inference
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.

teachings.teaching: Inference from one part to another part is valid because the inferred entity is one and the same, known through its parts.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the unity of the observer and the observed; the part reveals the whole through direct perception.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (part 19) discusses the nature of inference (anumana), focusing on whether it apprehends a universal (sāmānya) or a particular (viśeṣa). The text argues that inference involves a part (ekadeśī) that is both the object and the means of inference, and that the relationship between the inferential mark and the inferred property is grasped through a specific instance, not a universal. It critiques the view that inference only establishes a universal, showing that it leads to the fallacy of proving what is already known (siddhasādhyatā). The chapter concludes that inference must be about a particular qualified by a universal, and that the relationship is understood through a single instance without needing a universal.

story.title: The Nature of Inference: Part and Whole
story.arc: Dialectical analysis of the scope of inference, refuting the view that it only establishes universals and establishing that it involves a particular qualified by a universal.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely from the fifth chapter (adhyāya 5), pada 4.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical philosophical debate with abstract concepts.

teachings.teaching: Inference (anumāna) involves a part (ekadeśī) that serves both as the object and the means of inference.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle that the practitioner must focus on a specific aspect (ekadeśī) of the practice, which simultaneously reveals the whole and is the means to realize it.

teachings.teaching: The relationship between the inferential mark and the inferred property is grasped through a single instance, not through a universal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct experience (pratyakṣa) over abstract universals; this teaching supports the idea that knowledge arises from concrete, individual instances of practice.

teachings.teaching: If inference only established a universal, it would be proving what is already known (siddhasādhyatā).
teachings.lens_note: This warns against intellectual knowledge that does not lead to new realization; in Kriya-Yoga, mere theoretical understanding is insufficient without transformative practice.

teachings.teaching: Inference must be about a particular qualified by a universal, not a bare universal or a bare particular.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework integrates the universal (sāmānya) and particular (viśeṣa) in the act of knowing, mirroring the integration of theory and practice in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (5.4.30-33) discusses the nature of inference (anumana), specifically arguing that the relation (sambandha) between the logical mark (linga) and the inferred property is not directly inferred but is understood implicitly through the qualification of a subject. The text refutes the idea that relation itself can be a probandum, using examples like 'the mountain has fire' where the relation of fire to mountain is not separately inferred but is grasped as part of the qualified entity. The commentary elaborates on the role of the possessive suffix (matub) and compares it to words like 'dandī' (one with a staff), which denote a qualified entity and imply a relation without explicitly stating it.

story.title: Refutation of Relation as Probandum in Inference
story.arc: The chapter argues that in inference, the relation between the mark and the inferred property is not the object of inference; rather, it is implicitly understood when a qualified entity is inferred.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely composed around 200 BCE to 200 CE.

teachings.teaching: In inference, the relation (sambandha) is not directly inferred; it is understood implicitly when a qualified entity is inferred, e.g., 'the mountain has fire' implies fire's relation to the mountain without separately proving it.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would see this as emphasizing the practical, experiential nature of knowledge: the relation is not a separate object of knowledge but is realized in the act of perception or inference, aligning with the yoga principle of direct experience over theoretical abstraction.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (part 21) discusses the semantics of secondary suffixes like tva and tal, and the compound 'daṇḍin' (staff-bearer). It argues that the suffix does not denote the relation itself but the qualified entity (viśiṣṭa), with the relation being implied. The chapter refutes the view that the relation is the primary meaning, citing Panini's rules and the principle that a qualifier must be expressed first.

story.title: Mimamsa Sutras — Chapter 0 (part 21): Semantics of Secondary Suffixes
story.arc: Philosophical debate on word meaning
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger commentary on Mimamsa Sutras, likely post-Paninian.

teachings.teaching: The suffix in words like 'daṇḍin' denotes the qualified entity, not the relation itself; the relation is implied.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that action (kriya) is the qualifier that reveals the agent; the relation is inherent in the action.

teachings.teaching: A qualifier must be expressed first for the qualified to be understood.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as analogous to the priority of practice (kriya) over theoretical knowledge; the qualifier (action) must be established before the qualified (self) is realized.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana) and the relationship between the property-bearer (dharmin) and the property (dharma) in inferential reasoning. It argues that in a valid inference, the property (e.g., smoke) is predicated of the property-bearer (e.g., mountain) as a qualifier, not as a separate entity. The text critiques alternative views that treat the property as the primary subject, emphasizing that ordinary language and reasoning always present the property-bearer as qualified by the property.

story.title: Inference and the Property-Bearer
story.arc: Philosophical analysis of inference in Mimamsa
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, likely from the 5th chapter (Adhyaya 5, Pada 4).

teachings.teaching: In inference, the property (e.g., smoke) is always understood as qualifying the property-bearer (e.g., mountain), not as an independent entity. The relationship between them is one of qualifier-qualified, not a separate relation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would see this as emphasizing the practical unity of subject and predicate in action: the yogi's direct perception (pratyaksha) of the self (dharmin) is always qualified by its attributes (dharma), and inference (anumana) is a tool to clarify that unity, not to separate them.

teachings.teaching: The property-bearer (dharmin) is the primary subject of inference, and the property (dharma) is subordinate. This is evident in ordinary language: 'the mountain has fire' not 'fire is on the mountain'.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as aligning with the Kriya-Yoga principle that the self (atman) is the primary reality, and all qualities (gunas) are its subordinate expressions. Inference should lead to direct realization of the self, not mere intellectual analysis.

chapter_summary: This chapter discusses the logical structure of inference in Mimamsa, focusing on the relationship between the subject (dharmin) and property (dharma) in a syllogism. It critiques various interpretations of how the property is predicated, using the example of fire and its location. The text examines the proper formulation of the logical reason (hetu) and the fallacy of proving what is already established (siddhasādhyatā).

story.title: Analysis of Inference in Mimamsa
story.arc: Logical analysis of syllogistic reasoning
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger commentary on Mimamsa Sutras

teachings.teaching: The proper formulation of an inference requires that the reason (hetu) be stated without qualification to avoid ambiguity in the relationship between the subject and property.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need for precise action (kriya) without mental qualification, aligning with the practice of single-pointed focus.

teachings.teaching: The fallacy of siddhasādhyatā (proving what is already established) occurs when the subject is already known to possess the property, making the inference redundant.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga teaching that one should not waste effort on what is already realized; practice should be directed toward the unmanifest.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras (5.4.42-49) discusses the logical structure of inference (anumana), specifically how a property (dharma) like fire can be inferred from a locus (dharmin) like a mountain. It critiques various positions on whether the inferred object is the locus qualified by the property or the property itself, concluding that the locus (e.g., mountain) qualified by fire is the proper object of inference, not fire alone. The chapter engages with objections and alternative views, emphasizing that the property serves as a qualifier of the locus.

story.title: Inference of Fire from Locus
story.arc: Logical analysis of inference in Mimamsa epistemology
story.timeline_note: Part of the Mimamsa Sutras, Adhyaya 5, Pada 4
story.comic_potential: Low; dense philosophical debate with no narrative elements.

teachings.teaching: The object of inference is the locus (e.g., mountain) qualified by the property (e.g., fire), not the property alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that the ultimate reality (Brahman) is known through its qualities (saguna) but is not reducible to them. The practitioner infers the presence of the divine in the world through its attributes, but the locus (the Self) remains primary.

teachings.teaching: A property cannot be inferred without a locus; the locus is the substrate of the property.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the idea that spiritual experiences (properties) must be grounded in the stable awareness of the Self (locus). Without the Self, experiences are fleeting and lack foundation.

teachings.teaching: The inference of fire from smoke is valid only when the relation between them is known; the property (smoke) qualifies the locus (mountain) as having fire.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the relationship between the practitioner's effort (kriya) and spiritual attainment (yoga) must be understood through direct experience and scripture, not mere logic.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Mimamsa Sutras discusses the nature of inference (anumana), particularly the relationship between the inferential sign (linga) and the inferred property. It argues that inference is not merely about a generic property but about a specific qualified entity, and addresses objections regarding the scope and validity of inference.

story.title: Mimamsa Sutras — Chapter 0 (part 25)
story.arc: Epistemology of Inference
story.timeline_note: Part of a continuous commentary on Mimamsa Sutras.

teachings.teaching: Inference (anumana) has a qualified (viśiṣṭa) object, not a generic one.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that knowledge must be specific and actionable, not abstract.

teachings.teaching: The inferential sign (linga) operates through a previously known relation (pratibandha), not through mere generic association.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on experiential connection rather than theoretical knowledge.

teachings.teaching: The object of inference is not a generic property but a specific qualified entity, because the generic property is already known.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the need for precise, contextual understanding in practice.

teachings.teaching: The relationship between the inferential sign and the inferred property is not one of identity; they are distinct but connected.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga principle that means and ends are distinct yet inseparable in practice.

teachings.teaching: The validity of inference depends on the intention (vivakṣā) of the speaker, not on a fixed classification of means and ends.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the importance of intention (sankalpa) in determining the efficacy of practice.
